title: Power BI - Visualizations, Charts, and DAX Measures
description: All visual types, how to configure them, DAX measure patterns including time intelligence

visual_types.name: Clustered Bar Chart
visual_types.use_case: Compare values across categories; categories on Y-axis, values on X-axis. Best when category labels are long.
visual_types.notes: Horizontal orientation. Use when you have many categories that would crowd a column chart.

visual_types.name: Stacked Bar Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show both total and segment composition. Each bar is divided by Legend field.
visual_types.notes: Harder to compare individual segments across categories; use when total is as important as breakdown.

visual_types.name: 100% Stacked Bar Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show proportional composition without regard for absolute totals. Always fills to 100%.

visual_types.name: Clustered Column Chart
visual_types.use_case: Compare values across categories or time periods; categories on X-axis. Best for time-based comparisons.
visual_types.notes: Vertical orientation. Most common chart type for monthly totals by category.

visual_types.name: Stacked Column Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show total and breakdown by segment over time.

visual_types.name: 100% Stacked Column Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show proportional composition over time. Good for showing share shifts month-over-month.

visual_types.name: Line Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show continuous change over time. Ideal for monthly trends, moving averages, multiple KPIs on one chart.
visual_types.notes: Best for continuous data. Add forecast or anomaly detection via Analytics pane.

visual_types.name: Area Chart
visual_types.use_case: Line chart with filled area under the line. Emphasizes magnitude of change and cumulative values.
visual_types.notes: Stacked area chart useful for showing component contributions to a total trend.

visual_types.name: Combo Chart (Line + Column)
visual_types.use_case: Compare two measures with different scales on one visual. Common pattern: bars for volume, line for rate/percentage.
visual_types.notes: Has two independent Y axes. Use when one measure dominates scale and hides the other in separate charts.

visual_types.name: Ribbon Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show rank changes over time. The category with highest value always appears at top for each period.

visual_types.name: Waterfall Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show how an initial value is affected by positive and negative changes, arriving at a final value. Ideal for budget variance, P&L bridges.
visual_types.notes: Automatically colors increases green, decreases red, and totals blue (configurable).

visual_types.name: Funnel Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show sequential stages of a process with drop-off between stages. Sales pipeline, conversion rates.

visual_types.name: Pie Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show proportion of a whole for a small number of categories (< 6 ideally).
visual_types.notes: Avoid for many categories or similar-sized slices — use Treemap or Bar chart instead.

visual_types.name: Donut Chart
visual_types.use_case: Same as Pie but with a hollow center that can show a total or label.

visual_types.name: Treemap
visual_types.use_case: Display hierarchical data as nested rectangles. Size = value. Good for many categories.

visual_types.name: Scatter Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show relationship/correlation between two numerical measures. Each point is one entity.
visual_types.notes: Add a Bubble chart by placing a measure in Size. Add trend line via Analytics pane.

visual_types.name: Card
visual_types.use_case: Display a single key metric prominently. No axes or categories.
visual_types.notes: Use for KPIs at the top of a dashboard. Apply conditional formatting to change color based on value.

visual_types.name: Multi-row Card
visual_types.use_case: Show multiple measures or a category list in a compact card layout.

visual_types.name: KPI Visual
visual_types.use_case: Show a measure, its trend over time, and progress toward a target. Displays current value, trend indicator, and target.

visual_types.name: Gauge Chart
visual_types.use_case: Show a single value's progress between minimum and maximum, with a target needle.
visual_types.notes: Good for showing current attainment vs target (e.g. 72% of monthly quota).

visual_types.name: Table
visual_types.use_case: Display exact values in rows and columns. Best for precise comparisons where exact numbers matter.
field_wells: Columns (any number of dimensions and measures)
visual_types.notes: Add conditional formatting for data bars, background colors, or icons per column.

visual_types.name: Matrix
visual_types.use_case: Cross-tab showing measures broken down by two dimensions (rows and columns). Supports drill-down hierarchies.
visual_types.notes: Equivalent to a pivot table. Supports subtotals, grand totals, and conditional formatting.

visual_types.name: Slicer
visual_types.use_case: On-canvas filter control that updates all other visuals on the page (or synced pages).

visual_types.name: Map / Filled Map
visual_types.use_case: Show data by geographic location. Map = bubble markers. Filled Map = shaded regions by country/state.
field_wells: Location (place name, lat/long, or geographic hierarchy)

visual_types.name: Decomposition Tree
visual_types.use_case: Interactively drill down into a measure by dimensions in any order. AI auto-selects highest-impact next split.

visual_types.name: Key Influencers
visual_types.use_case: Automatically identify which factors most influence a selected metric or outcome.

visual_types.name: Smart Narrative
visual_types.use_case: Auto-generated text summary of the report data. Updates dynamically with slicer selections.
field_wells: Any fields from the data model

visual_types.name: R Visual / Python Visual
visual_types.use_case: Run R or Python scripts to create any visualisation, statistical model, or forecast.
field_wells: Values (passed as dataframe to the script)

visual_configuration.adding_visual: Click the visual icon in the Visualizations pane. A placeholder appears on the canvas. Drag fields from the Data pane onto the field wells in the Visualizations pane, or drag directly onto the canvas placeholder.

field_wells.axis: The categorical or time dimension on the X or Y axis (e.g. DateTable[YearMonth], Product[Category])
field_wells.values: The numeric measures to display (e.g. [Total Sales], [MoM Change %])
field_wells.legend: Groups the values into coloured series (e.g. Region, Product Category)
field_wells.tooltips: Additional measures shown in the hover tooltip but not plotted on the chart
field_wells.filters: Visual-level filters applied only to this visual (not the page)

format_pane.description: Access via the paint roller icon in the Visualizations pane. Controls all styling.
key_sections: General: title, subtitle, background, border, shadow, responsive
Visual: X/Y axis, data labels, legend, plot area, data colors, reference lines
X Axis: title, range (min/max), gridlines, label rotation
Y Axis: title, range, gridlines, scale (linear vs logarithmic)
Data labels: position, font size, format
Conditional formatting: rules for colors, icons, data bars based on measure values

visual_configuration.filters_pane: Apply Filters at three levels: Visual level (only this visual), Page level (all visuals on this page), Report level (all visuals in all pages). Drag any field into the Filters pane and configure filter type (Basic, Advanced, Top N).
visual_configuration.cross_filtering: By default, selecting a data point in one visual cross-filters all other visuals on the page. Control this in Format > Edit interactions (Pro feature): choose Filter, Highlight, or None for each visual pair.
visual_configuration.drill_through: Right-click a data point > Drill through > target page. The target page shows data filtered to the selected value. Add drill-through filters on the target page by dragging fields to the Drill through field well.
visual_configuration.bookmarks: Capture current state of visuals/filters as a bookmark. Use buttons linked to bookmarks for interactive storytelling or reset-filter buttons.

dax_fundamentals.measure_vs_column: Use MEASURES for aggregations and calculations that depend on filter context (what's selected in slicers). Use CALCULATED COLUMNS for row-level lookups and transformations that don't depend on report filters.

aggregation_functions.COUNTA: COUNTA(table[column]) — count of non-blank values (any data type)
aggregation_functions.COUNTROWS: COUNTROWS(table) — count rows in table (often used with FILTER)
aggregation_functions.SUMX: SUMX(table, expression) — row-by-row evaluation then sum. Example: SUMX(Sales, Sales[Qty] * Sales[Price])
aggregation_functions.COUNTX: COUNTX(table, expression) — row-by-row then count non-blank results

calculate.description: CALCULATE is the most important DAX function. It evaluates an expression in a modified filter context.

examples.simple_filter:
// Sales for Electronics category only
Electronics Sales = CALCULATE([Total Sales], Product[Category] = "Electronics")
examples.date_filter:
// Sales in 2024
Sales 2024 = CALCULATE([Total Sales], YEAR('DateTable'[Date]) = 2024)
examples.remove_filter:
// Sales ignoring any Region slicer selection
All Regions Sales = CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(Sales[Region]))
examples.multiple_filters:
// 2024 Electronics Sales
Electronics 2024 = CALCULATE([Total Sales], Product[Category] = "Electronics", YEAR('DateTable'[Date]) = 2024)

filter_removal_functions.ALL: ALL(table) or ALL(table[column]) — removes all filters from a table or column. Used for % of total calculations.
filter_removal_functions.ALLEXCEPT: ALLEXCEPT(table, table[col1], table[col2]) — removes all filters EXCEPT the specified columns
filter_removal_functions.ALLSELECTED: ALLSELECTED([table/column]) — removes filters from visuals while keeping slicer/filter selections
filter_removal_functions.REMOVEFILTERS: REMOVEFILTERS(table/column) — explicit alias for ALL, more readable
filter_removal_functions.example_percent_of_total:
% of Total =
    DIVIDE(
        [Total Sales],
        CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALL(Sales))
    )
filter_removal_functions.example_percent_of_category:
% of Category =
    DIVIDE(
        [Total Sales],
        CALCULATE([Total Sales], ALLEXCEPT(Sales, Sales[Category]))
    )

logical_and_conditional.IF:
IF(condition, value_if_true, value_if_false)
Example: IF([MoM Change %] > 0, "Growing", "Declining")
logical_and_conditional.SWITCH:
SWITCH(expression, value1, result1, value2, result2, ..., else_result)
Example: SWITCH(TRUE(), [Score] >= 90, "A", [Score] >= 80, "B", "C")
logical_and_conditional.ISBLANK: ISBLANK(value) — returns TRUE if value is blank
logical_and_conditional.COALESCE: COALESCE(value1, value2, ...) — returns first non-blank value
logical_and_conditional.IFERROR: IFERROR(expression, alternate) — returns alternate if expression errors
logical_and_conditional.AND_OR:
AND(condition1, condition2) or use && operator
OR(condition1, condition2) or use || operator

text_and_format_functions.FORMAT:
FORMAT(value, format_string) — converts to text with formatting
FORMAT([Total Sales], "$#,##0")  -- $1,234
FORMAT([MoM Change %], "0.0%")   -- 12.3%
FORMAT(MAX('DateTable'[Date]), "MMMM YYYY")  -- January 2024
text_and_format_functions.LEFT_RIGHT_MID: LEFT(text, n), RIGHT(text, n), MID(text, start, length)

relationship_functions.RELATED:
RELATED(table[column]) — follows a many-to-one relationship to bring a value from a related table into the current row context. Used in calculated columns.
Example (in Sales table): Sales[Category] = RELATED(Product[Category])
relationship_functions.RELATEDTABLE:
RELATEDTABLE(table) — returns all rows in a related table that match the current row context. Used in measures.
Example: OrderCount = COUNTROWS(RELATEDTABLE(OrderDetails))
relationship_functions.USERELATIONSHIP: CALCULATE([Total Sales], USERELATIONSHIP(Sales[ShipDate], 'DateTable'[Date])) — activates an inactive relationship

variable_usage.description: VAR/RETURN makes DAX measures more readable and can improve performance by evaluating an expression once.
variable_usage.syntax:
MeasureName =
VAR variable1 = expression1
VAR variable2 = expression2
RETURN
    IF(ISBLANK(variable1), BLANK(), DIVIDE(variable1 - variable2, variable2))
variable_usage.best_practice: Use VAR for any value referenced more than once in a measure. Avoids recalculation and improves readability.

dax_time_intelligence.requirement: Requires a Date table marked as a Date Table, with a continuous Date column covering full calendar years.

complete_measures.previous_month:
Sales Previous Month =
    CALCULATE(
        [Total Sales],
        PREVIOUSMONTH('DateTable'[Date])
    )
complete_measures.mom_change_pct:
MoM Change % =
    VAR CurrentMonth = [Total Sales]
    VAR PrevMonth    = [Sales Previous Month]
    RETURN
        IF(
            ISBLANK(PrevMonth) || PrevMonth = 0,
            BLANK(),
            DIVIDE(CurrentMonth - PrevMonth, PrevMonth)
        )
complete_measures.yoy_pct:
YoY Growth % =
    VAR Current = [Total Sales]
    VAR Prior   = [Sales SPLY]
    RETURN
        IF(
            ISBLANK(Prior) || Prior = 0,
            BLANK(),
            DIVIDE(Current - Prior, Prior)
        )
complete_measures.last_3_months:
Sales Last 3 Months =
    CALCULATE(
        [Total Sales],
        DATESINPERIOD(
            'DateTable'[Date],
            LASTDATE('DateTable'[Date]),
            -3,
            MONTH
        )
    )
complete_measures.running_total:
Sales Running Total =
    CALCULATE(
        [Total Sales],
        FILTER(
            ALL('DateTable'[YearMonth]),
            'DateTable'[YearMonth] <= MAX('DateTable'[YearMonth])
        )
    )
complete_measures.previous_year_ytd:
Sales PY YTD =
    CALCULATE(
        [Sales YTD],
        SAMEPERIODLASTYEAR('DateTable'[Date])
    )
complete_measures.moving_average_3m:
3-Month Moving Avg =
    VAR AvgResult = AVERAGEX(
        DATESINPERIOD('DateTable'[Date], LASTDATE('DateTable'[Date]), -3, MONTH),
        [Total Sales]
    )
    RETURN IF(ISBLANK([Total Sales]), BLANK(), AvgResult)

time_intelligence_function_reference.description: All dates from the previous month
All dates from the previous quarter
All dates from the previous year
All dates from the next month
Same period shifted back 1 year
Shift dates by n intervals (DAY, MONTH, QUARTER, YEAR)
Dates in a rolling window of n intervals
Dates between two fixed dates
Year-to-date dates
Month-to-date dates
Quarter-to-date dates
Year-to-date aggregate. Shortcut for CALCULATE(expr, DATESYTD(dates))
Month-to-date aggregate
Quarter-to-date aggregate
Value at last date of the current month
Value at first date of the current month
First date in current context
Last date in current context
First date of the current month
Last date of the current month

relationships.description: Relationships link tables so filters propagate from one table to another.

types.direction: Single — filters flow from the 'one' side to the 'many' side
types.example: DateTable[Date] (1) -> Sales[OrderDate] (*). Slicing by month in a report filters Sales rows.
types.recommendation: This is the standard pattern in a star schema. Always preferred.

types.example: Used for bridge tables (e.g. products that belong to multiple categories)
types.warning: Avoid bidirectional many-to-many where possible — causes unexpected cross-filtering and ambiguity errors.

setup_steps: 1. Open Model view in Power BI Desktop
3. Power BI creates the relationship. Verify cardinality (many-to-one) and cross-filter direction (single)
4. Active relationships are shown as solid lines; inactive as dashed lines
5. Only one relationship can be active between two tables — use USERELATIONSHIP() to activate others in specific measures
relationships.star_schema_pattern: Best practice: use a star schema with one or more fact tables (large, many rows: Sales, Events) connected to dimension tables (smaller, lookup tables: Date, Product, Customer, Geography). All relationships radiate outward from fact tables to dimensions like a star.

report_design_tips.layout: Use a consistent grid. Title at top, KPI cards below, main chart in the middle, detail table or filters at bottom. Align visuals using Format > Align.
report_design_tips.color_palette: Limit to 3-5 colors. Use one highlight color for the current period, one for comparison. Apply a theme JSON for consistency across all visuals.
report_design_tips.titles: Every visual should have a descriptive title. Consider dynamic titles using DAX: Visual Title = "Sales by Month — " & SELECTEDVALUE('DateTable'[Year], "All Years")
report_design_tips.data_labels: Show data labels only on key visuals (cards, KPIs). Hide labels on dense charts to avoid clutter.
report_design_tips.conditional_formatting: Apply to tables and matrices: green/red background or arrows for MoM Change %. Format > Cell elements > Icon or Background color > Rules.
report_design_tips.tooltips: Add a second report page as a tooltip page. Set its size to Tooltip in Page Information. Then assign it to visuals via Format > Tooltips > Page.
report_design_tips.accessibility: Use alt text on visuals (Format > General > Alt text). Ensure color contrast meets WCAG guidelines. Provide a table as an alternative to charts for screen reader users.
report_design_tips.performance: Avoid calculated columns on large tables — use measures instead. Avoid DISTINCTCOUNT on large high-cardinality columns. Use Import mode over DirectQuery for better performance.
report_design_tips.bookmarks_and_buttons: Create a 'Reset filters' button: add a bookmark with no filters active, create a Button visual, set Action > Bookmark to the reset bookmark.
